instruction: Classify the candidate note into a hiring-stage recommendation.
baseline_prompt: You are a recruiting screening assistant. Classify the candidate note into a hiring-stage recommendation. Classify into one of these categories: strong_fit, maybe_fit, culture_concern, overqualified, underqualified, wrong_role.
source: Authored for TEI-Bench (Sonnet-generated, Sonnet-validated; kept only label-agreed examples).

train.query: Conducted final round with Michael Torres for Regional Sales Director. His track record speaks for itself: exceeded quota 11 of the last 12 quarters, built a team from 3 to 15 reps, and has existing relationships with 4 of our target enterprise accounts. His strategic vision for territory expansion matches our growth plan perfectly. Negotiation style is consultative rather than aggressive, which fits our buyer demographic. He can relocate to Denver by month-end.

Solid background in customer success with good metrics to show for it. The concern is that most of their experience has been with B2C rather than our B2B enterprise focus. They seemed to understand the difference when I explained our sales cycle, but it's hard to gauge if they can make that transition smoothly. Recommend moving forward with a practical exercise to see how they approach enterprise account management scenarios.

The candidate has solid Java experience and completed the technical assessment successfully, but during the behavioral interview they struggled to articulate examples of teamwork. When asked about handling disagreements with colleagues, they seemed dismissive and stated they prefer to work independently without interference. Their communication style came across as somewhat abrasive.

Application received for entry-level marketing coordinator. Resume shows they were Global Brand Director at a major consumer goods company, managed $50M advertising budgets, and led a team of 30 across multiple countries. They have credentials and experience that align with our CMO role, not a coordinator position that involves social media scheduling and event logistics support.

Reviewing for Head of Marketing role. Candidate's experience is limited to social media coordination and content creation for a local nonprofit. We require someone with 10+ years leading marketing strategy, brand positioning, team leadership of 15+ people, and multi-million dollar budget management. This is an entry to mid-level profile for an executive position.

This applicant would be perfect for our data analyst team rather than the marketing coordinator position they applied for. Their entire background is in SQL, Tableau, and statistical modeling with zero marketing campaign experience. They seem to have misunderstood the job description.

Second interview with Amanda Wilson for the UX Researcher position went exceptionally well. She walked us through three case studies showing end-to-end research projects—from stakeholder interviews through usability testing to actionable recommendations. Her mixed-methods approach and familiarity with UserTesting and Optimal Workshop tools match our tech stack. She asked insightful questions about how research informs product decisions here. PhD in Human-Computer Interaction is a bonus. References confirmed she's collaborative and detail-oriented.

The candidate brings relevant industry knowledge and has worked on similar problems, though at a different part of the value chain. They'd need some onboarding to understand our specific processes and systems, which could take a few months. Their attitude and problem-solving approach during the interview were promising, but I'm not certain if the transition effort is worth it versus continuing our search. Would benefit from a conversation with someone who knows the technical requirements more intimately.

Strong technical competencies in cybersecurity and relevant certifications. The concern is around emotional intelligence - the candidate became noticeably defensive when asked about a project failure and blamed team members exclusively. They also expressed impatience with mentoring junior staff, which is a key part of this role given our collaborative engineering culture.

Interviewed for administrative assistant position. Candidate has 20 years as an Executive Assistant supporting C-level executives at major corporations, including managing complex international travel, coordinating board meetings, and handling confidential strategic matters. This role supports a middle manager with basic scheduling and expense reports. They'd likely find the work significantly below their capability level.

Applicant for Nurse Practitioner role only holds an RN license and associate degree. Position explicitly requires MSN and NP certification with prescriptive authority. While clinical bedside experience is solid, they don't meet the minimum credentialing requirements to practice in this capacity.

Impressive graphic design portfolio and Adobe Creative Suite mastery, however we need a UX researcher who can conduct user interviews and analyze behavioral data. Their skillset doesn't match our research-focused requirements.

Video interview with Lin Park for Content Strategist position exceeded expectations. Her writing samples demonstrate the versatility we need—from technical white papers to engaging social content. She's managed editorial calendars for B2B SaaS companies with similar audience profiles. SEO knowledge is current, and she provided specific examples of content that drove measurable pipeline growth. Culture-add potential is high; her questions revealed alignment with our values around transparency and continuous learning. Seeking $72k which is mid-range for the role.

This person has the right educational credentials and about 70% of the technical skills we're looking for. They're missing experience with our cloud infrastructure stack, but they've worked with similar technologies and claim to be fast learners. References were positive but didn't speak specifically to the gaps we identified. I think a technical panel interview would help us determine if the learning curve is manageable or if we need someone more plug-and-play.

This candidate meets all the requirements on paper with 5 years of marketing experience and a proven track record. During the panel interview, though, they appeared inflexible when discussing our remote-first work environment, expressing strong preference for in-office presence and skepticism about distributed teams. Their responses suggested difficulty adapting to our values around work-life balance.

test.query: Candidate applying for warehouse associate position has 12 years as Director of Supply Chain Operations, implemented ERP systems across multi-site operations, and managed logistics budgets exceeding $100M annually. They hold Six Sigma Black Belt and an advanced degree in Operations Management. This hourly position involves picking, packing, and inventory tasks that wouldn't utilize their strategic expertise.

Candidate has 6 months of customer service experience but the position requires 3+ years in B2B sales with proven track record of closing enterprise deals. No evidence of any sales methodology training or quota achievement. Friendly personality but lacks the fundamental sales competencies we need.

Candidate has exceptional backend engineering skills with 8 years of Java and Python experience, but we're hiring for a frontend React developer. Their portfolio shows no JavaScript framework work. They mentioned interest in transitioning but lack the required skills for this specific opening.

Evaluated candidate for Project Manager role in our construction division. He holds PMP certification, has managed 12+ commercial building projects ranging from $2M to $15M, and maintained an average 96% on-time completion rate. Familiar with Procore and our preferred project management methodology. His conflict resolution example during the panel interview demonstrated maturity and client-first thinking. Drug test and background check already cleared. His 20 years of experience brings stability without rigidity.

The candidate has 3 years of experience in project coordination, which is close to what we need, though they haven't directly managed the specific software platforms we use. Their communication skills during the phone screen were excellent, and they showed genuine enthusiasm for the role. However, I'd like the hiring manager to probe deeper into their ability to handle our scale of operations, as their previous company was significantly smaller. Worth a second conversation to assess technical adaptability.

Technical skills are definitely there - strong background in data analysis and Python. However, multiple interviewers noted that the candidate interrupted frequently and didn't seem to listen to questions fully before responding. They also made several comments about their previous workplace being 'too collaborative' and needing 'less hand-holding from management.'

Reviewed application for customer service representative role. Applicant holds an MBA from Wharton and spent the last decade as VP of Customer Experience at two Fortune 500 companies, leading transformation initiatives and reporting directly to C-suite. Their salary history is 3-4x our budgeted range. Concerned they're applying during a job search gap and will leave as soon as a director-level opportunity emerges.

Candidate expressed interest in the Construction Project Manager position but has only worked as a general laborer and foreman on small residential projects. Role demands PMP certification, experience managing commercial builds over $10M, and vendor/subcontractor coordination for multi-phase developments. Foundation is there but nowhere near the level we need.

This person has deep expertise in supply chain logistics and warehouse operations management, which would be valuable for our operations team. However, they applied for the customer success manager position which requires client relationship skills they haven't demonstrated.

Phone screened candidate for the Full Stack Developer role. She has 4 years building React/Node.js applications, contributed to open source projects, and her GitHub shows clean, well-documented code. Solved our technical challenge in 35 minutes with an elegant solution. Currently works at a similar-sized startup and understands the pace. Her motivation for leaving is legitimate—seeking more backend infrastructure work, which is exactly what this position offers.

Interviewed someone with a non-traditional background who has transferable skills from adjacent fields. They demonstrated strong critical thinking and picked up on nuances quickly during our discussion, but they lack direct domain expertise that other candidates might bring. The salary expectations are reasonable and they're clearly motivated to pivot into this space. It's a bit of a gamble, so I'd suggest another round focused on assessing how steep the learning curve would actually be.

The candidate's project management experience aligns well with the job description and they have the certifications we're looking for. What's concerning is their response to questions about feedback - they stated they don't typically need much direction and seemed resistant to our quarterly review process. Their previous tenure history shows a pattern of leaving roles after 12-18 months when they felt 'micromanaged.'

Candidate's resume shows they were Chief Technology Officer at a Series C startup, previously Principal Engineer at Google, and hold a PhD in Computer Science from MIT. They've published 20+ papers and hold several patents. Our opening is for a junior backend developer position requiring 1-3 years experience. The gap between their credentials and this role is substantial.

Interviewed for the Machine Learning Engineer opening. Candidate completed an online bootcamp and has built a few personal projects with scikit-learn. However, the position requires advanced degree in Computer Science or related field, plus 5+ years deploying production ML systems. Not ready for this senior-level position.

Strong project management credentials with PMP certification and 10 years leading cross-functional teams, but they applied for an individual contributor software engineering role. Their technical skills are outdated and they clearly excel at coordination rather than coding.

Interviewed Sarah Chen for the Senior Data Analyst position. She has 6 years of experience with SQL, Python, and Tableau—exactly what we need. Her portfolio demonstrated complex dashboard projects for Fortune 500 clients. She explained her approach to A/B testing clearly and showed genuine enthusiasm about our mission. References were glowing. Salary expectations align with our range at $95-105k. Available to start in 3 weeks.

Candidate performed well on the coding challenge and has impressive credentials. However, during the lunch interview, they made several offhand remarks that suggested a lack of awareness around inclusive language. When the team discussed our commitment to diversity initiatives, the candidate seemed disengaged and changed the subject quickly.

This candidate has 15 years of senior leadership experience managing teams of 50+ engineers, including P&L responsibility for a $200M division. The open position is for an individual contributor software developer role with no management responsibilities. While technically skilled, their background suggests they would quickly become disengaged with the scope and compensation level of this junior-to-mid level position.
